summary:
The Taiping Heavenly Kingdom (1851–64) was the millenarian Christian theocratic state established by Hong Xiuquan in southern China, centered on the conquered city of Nanjing (renamed Tianjing, the 'Heavenly Capital'), that sustained the [[event:2543|Taiping Rebellion]] against the [[event:2337|Qing dynasty]] for over a decade. It was one of the most remarkable political experiments of the nineteenth century: a state organized around a syncretic theology in which Hong Xiuquan claimed to be the younger brother of Jesus Christ, with radical social programs including land redistribution, gender equality in principle, prohibition of foot binding, and suppression of opium and alcohol.

At its height the Taiping Heavenly Kingdom controlled substantial territories in the Yangtze River valley and fielded armies in the millions. The kingdom had a structured bureaucracy organized on theocratic lines, issued its own currency, and governed a population of tens of millions. Its social radicalism — including the formation of separate male and female armies and the theoretical abolition of private property — represented a genuine alternative vision to the Confucian social order, however imperfectly realized in practice.

The kingdom was ultimately destroyed by a combination of Qing forces and the Ever Victorious Army — a Chinese force with Western officers, most famously 'Chinese' Gordon — along with the Hunan Army (Xiang Army) raised by Zeng Guofan. Internal factional conflict among the Taiping leadership, particularly the Incident at Tianjing in 1856 when Hong Xiuquan's subordinates massacred thousands of each other's followers, severely weakened the movement. Nanjing fell in July 1864; Hong Xiuquan had died weeks earlier. The total death toll of the Taiping period, from warfare, famine, and displacement, is estimated at 20–30 million or more, making it one of the deadliest conflicts in human history.
